data.title: Gold And S&P 500 Both Volatile
data.description:
It’s been a volatile year for stocks so far, but more surprising is that gold prices, which have rallied more than 25% YTD, have been even more volatile. Year to date, the SPDR Gold Trust (GLD) has experienced a daily move of at least 1% on 40% of all trading days compared to 37% for the S&P 500, as measured by the SPDR S&P 500 ETF (SPY).

The chart below shows the rolling 50-day average number of daily 1% moves in GLD over the last 20 years. The current level of 54% is modestly below the recent high of 56%, and that was the highest frequency of daily 1% moves since late 2011! The second chart shows the same calculation for SPY over the last 20 years. While the current level of 42% is hardly extreme relative to history, earlier this month it was at 52%.

In both charts, areas where the line shifts from blue to red indicate periods when both GLD and SPY had 1%+ daily moves on more than 40% of trading days in a rolling 50-day period. The only periods in the last 20 years when both ETFs simultaneously had 1%+ daily moves over 50 days were during the Financial Crisis, late 2011 after S&P downgraded the US AAA credit rating, early 2016, Covid, and now.

The chart below shows the performance of GLD over the last 20 years, and here again, the red line indicates the periods when both GLD and SPY had 1%+ daily moves at least 40% of the time over a 50-day span. For GLD, there was no consistent pattern of performance leading up to or after these periods.

For the S&P 500, however, the pattern surrounding these occurrences was more consistent. Except the Financial crisis when elevated levels of volatility in both ETFs started just after the S&P 500’s peak and continued for most of the bear market, in the three other periods (and the current period), it wasn’t until late in the decline or after the market made its low that the average number of 1% daily moves in each ETF exceeded 40%.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: The State Of REITs: May 2025 Edition
data.description:
REIT Performance

REITs fell further into the red in 2025 during a tough April selloff, with a -6.45% average total return. The REIT sector badly underperformed the broader market, falling far short of the Dow Jones Industrial Average (-3.1%), S&P 500 (-0.7%) and NASDAQ (+0.9%). The market cap weighted Vanguard Real Estate ETF (VNQ) handily outpaced the average REIT again in April (-2.44% vs. -6.45%) and has massively outperformed year-to-date (+0.19% vs. -9.10%). The spread between the 2025 FFO multiples of large-cap REITs (17.5x) and small cap REITs (12.9x) narrowed in March as multiples contracted 0.6 turns for large caps but held steady for small caps. Investors currently need to pay an average of 35.7% more for each dollar of FFO from large-cap REITs relative to small-cap REITs. In this monthly publication, I will provide REIT data on numerous metrics to help readers identify which property types and individual securities currently offer the best opportunities to achieve their investment goals.

Microcap REITs (-8.87%) underperformed their larger peers for the 6th straight month. Large caps (-2.93%) continued to outperform both mid-caps (-5.45%) and small caps (-8.69%) in April. During the first four months of 2025, large-cap REITs have outperformed small caps by 1081 basis points.

16 out of 18 Property Types Averaged Negative Returns in April

Only 11.11% of REIT property types averaged a positive total return in April. There was a 20.17% total return spread between the best- and worst-performing property types. Data Centers (+7.28%) and Infrastructure (+2.47%) were the only property types in positive territory in April. Timber (-12.90%), Mall (-10.52%) and Land REITs (-10.10%) averaged double-digit declines.

Office (-24.06%) and Hotel REITs (-22.90%) significantly underperformed over the first four months of 2025. Health Care (+7.23%), Infrastructure (+6.88%), and Casinos (+6.00%) were the only three property types to average positive total returns year-to-date.

The REIT sector as a whole saw the average P/FFO (2025Y) decrease 0.5 turns in April from 13.9x down to 13.4x. 16.7% of property types averaged multiple expansion and 83.3% averaged multiple contraction in April. Data Centers (26.9x), Multifamily (24.6x), Land (21.6x), Single Family Housing (19.1x), and Infrastructure (18.7x) currently trade at the highest average multiples among REIT property types. Hotels (5.9x) and Office (8.2x) are the only property types that average single-digit FFO multiples.

Performance of Individual Securities

Equity Commonwealth (EQC) has delisted from the NYSE with a last trading day of April 21st. EQC paid out a final cash liquidating distribution of $1.60 per share on April 22nd, which followed a $19.00/share cash liquidating distribution in December.

Digital Realty Trust (DLR) (+12.04%) achieved a strong gain in April as they raised guidance and outlined an ambitious growth plan for the year. Despite outpacing all other REITs in April, DLR is still in the red year-to-date, with a -8.72% total return in 2025.

Wheeler REIT (WHLR) (-63.61%) was yet again the worst-performing REIT in April and has already plummeted a disastrous -98.29% in just the first 4 months of 2025. This collapse mirrors Wheeler’s -98.47% 2024 return as the free fall continues.

Only 15.38% of REITs had a positive total return in April. REITs have averaged a -9.10% year-to-date total return in 2025, which was a bit worse than the -7.65% return for the REIT sector over the first 4 months of 2024.

For the convenience of reading this table in a larger font, the table below is available as a PDF as well.

Dividend Yield

The dividend yield is an important component of a REIT's total return. The particularly high dividend yields of the REIT sector are, for many investors, the primary reason for investment in this sector. As many REITs are currently trading at share prices well below their NAV, yields are currently quite high for many REITs within the sector. Although a particularly high yield for a REIT may sometimes reflect a disproportionately high risk, there exist opportunities in some cases to capitalize on dividend yields that are sufficiently attractive to justify the underlying risks of the investment. I have included below a table ranking equity REITs from the highest dividend yield (as of 4/30/2025) to the lowest dividend yield.

For the convenience of reading this table in a larger font, the table below is available as a PDF as well.

Although a REIT’s decision regarding whether to pay a quarterly dividend or a monthly dividend does not reflect on the quality of the company’s fundamentals or operations, a monthly dividend allows for smoother cash flow to the investor. Below is a list of equity REITs that pay monthly dividends, ranked from the highest yield to the lowest yield.

Dividend News

5 REITs announced increases to their dividend in April, 4 of which pay quarterly dividends and 1 of which pays monthly. Tanger (SKT) (+6.4%) announced the largest dividend raise, followed by UMH Properties (UMH) (+4.7%) and Medalist Diversified REIT (MDRR) (+3.8%). In total, 40 REITs increased their dividend during the first four months of 2025.

Economic Health

The number of corporate bankruptcies remained elevated and flat month over month in April. There were more filings year-to-date in 2025 than in the first 4 months of any other year since 2010. There have been 19.4% more bankruptcy filings over the first 4 months of 2025 than during the same period in 2024.

Valuation

REIT Premium/Discount to NAV by Property Type

Below is a downloadable data table, which ranks REITs within each property type from the largest discount to the largest premium to NAV. The consensus NAV used for this table is the average of analyst NAV estimates for each REIT. Both the NAV and the share price will change over time, so I will continue to include this table in upcoming issues of The State of REITs with updated consensus NAV estimates for each REIT for which such an estimate is available.

For the convenience of reading this table in a larger font, the table below is available as a PDF as well.

Takeaway

The large-cap REIT premium (relative to small cap REITs) narrowed in April and investors are now paying on average about 36% more for each dollar of 2025 FFO/share to buy large-cap REITs than small-cap REITs (17.5x/12.9x - 1 = 35.7%). As can be seen in the table below, there is presently a strong positive correlation between market cap and FFO multiple.

The table below shows the average NAV premium/discount of REITs of each market cap bucket. This data, much like the data for price/FFO, shows a strong, positive correlation between market cap and Price/NAV. The average large-cap REIT (-5.88%) trades at a single-digit discount to NAV, and mid-cap REITs (-12.60%) trade at a low double-digit discount. Small cap REITs (-30.04%) trade at just over 2/3 of NAV while micro caps (-45.01%) trade slightly north of half of their respective NAVs.

Much of the REIT Sector Has Still Not Fully Recovered from the Impact of Covid-19 and Lockdowns

Although the entire economy was impacted by Covid-19 and the corresponding government-imposed lockdowns, multiple real estate property types got hit particularly hard. Most notably, hotels were impacted by reduced travel and canceled events, office leasing was dealt a devastating blow by the shift to work-from-home and some retail was even banned from opening their doors to customers for a period of time.

One of the most direct victims of the economic hit REITs took was dividend payments. Even after multiple years of post-lockdown recovery, 42% of REITs still pay a lower dividend than they did pre-Covid and in some cases still have a suspended dividend. 50% or more of REITs still have a lower or suspended dividend in Hotels, Malls, Diversified, Office, Healthcare, Shopping Centers, and Advertising.

Only 2 hotel REITs have raised their dividend payments to greater than the level they were in 2019, Ryman Hospitality Properties (RHP) (+27.8%) and Host Hotels & Resorts (HST) (+5.9%). Others have cut their dividends by 20% - 98.1% or, in the case of Sotherly Hotels (SOHO) and Ashford Hospitality Trust (AHT), have suspended and not yet reinstated their dividends.

4 office REITs have suspended and not yet reinstated their dividends: Paramount Group (PGRE), Piedmont Office Realty Trust (PDM), Hudson Pacific Properties (HPP), and Creative Media & Community Trust (CMCT). Other Office REITs have cut their dividends anywhere from 15.3% to 98.2%. Not all Office REITs are the same, however, as Alexandria Real Estate Equities (ARE), which focuses on high-quality life science office space, has steadily increased earnings and raised their dividend by 28.2% over that same period.

Not all REITs have struggled to maintain their dividends, however, with many able to aggressively increase their dividends over the same period. The largest dividend hikes came from American Homes 4 Rent (AMH) (+500%), SBA Communications (SBAC) (+200%), and Rexford Industrial Realty (REXR) (+132.4%). Industrial accounts for 6 of the 10 REITs with the greatest dividend expansion since the end of 2019.

Equity REITs that currently pay a dividend are forecast by S&P Global Market Intelligence dividend analysts to increase their dividends steadily through 2029. This stable dividend growth is projected to be within a range of 1.9%-2.0% per year. Steady dividend growth can have a snowball effect, building income in a portfolio. Active investors have the opportunity to potentially achieve higher portfolio income growth, however, with heavier weightings in higher dividend growth REITs.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Real Estate Roundup: Why Office And Retail Have Made A Comeback
data.description:
The office and retail real estate sectors were losers during the pandemic, but changing trends are driving a comeback. MoneyTalk discusses with Colin Lynch, Managing Director and Head of Alternative Investments with TD Asset Management.

Transcript

Greg Bonnell: Work from home, online shopping, all the ways our lives changed in the past couple of years did weigh on office and retail real estate in recent years. But our guest today says both segments are showing some strength in today's environment. Joining us now to discuss, Colin Lynch, managing director and head of alternative investments with TD Asset Management.

Colin, always great to have you on the show. Excited to have you back.

Colin Lynch: Well, I'm excited to be here again. Thank you for having me.

Greg Bonnell: All right. So interesting things always in the real estate space. We have preconceived notions about how our lives have changed in the last several years, what it means for real estate.

I want to start with office, because office has struggled for a long time. Are there any green shoots?

Colin Lynch: Yeah, you're exactly right. Office has struggled for some time. But interestingly, we are seeing some green shoots, particularly when you look at what we call the fundamentals. So, what is leasing? How much leasing is being done? Are tenants taking more space? What rents are they paying? On those questions, we are seeing better answers today than we've seen in the last five years.

We are also seeing those answers over the course of 2024. Important to note, those better answers are concentrated in some of the highest-quality buildings. So we're still seeing that flight to quality, which makes a lot of sense. If you have the opportunity to upgrade your space, to move into newer space, better-located space, closer to transit, higher ESG environmental considerations-involved space, then you'll take that opportunity.

But what's new, I think, is the return-to-office mandates have begun working themselves through the system. And so when it comes to now tenants looking at their offices on those in-office days, we're seeing a growing number of tenants saying, I actually need a bit more space.

And so we're seeing some tenants beginning to take incremental adds to the space needs that they had, and you sum all of that up. And we're seeing more leasing across the portfolios in Canada, especially, but also in some cases internationally as well. So certainly some more optimism in the office space, more optimism than we've seen, I am sure, in the last three to four years, but importantly, concentrated in that highest-quality office space.

Greg Bonnell: More people back in the office, less seats for me on my morning commute, but that is my problem and not the market's problem. Retail has been interesting, right? Because I mean, obviously, there was a big push for online shopping. But I know in discussions you and I have had, retail has held up pretty well.

Colin Lynch: That's right. And you go back four or five years, and the discussion was all about death of retail. We'd sit at home, we'd order everything online, and we'd wait for those packages to arrive. And as we came out of the pandemic, what was interesting was that people began really looking forward to those in-person experiences.

And we saw right in '22, '23, people were staying longer, shopping more, their bags were heavier. And that has continued today. And so, when you look at performance, what are your returns by asset class in real estate in Canada, the trick question is, what was the best-returning major property type last year? It was retail.

And that is driven by some of those fundamentals that we were talking about in '23 and '24. We saw volumes recover very nicely. We saw shopping baskets fuller. And throughout the pandemic, as you know, we talked about the different types of retail. You had essential retail, which are grocery stores, bank branches and pharmacies, where we have to go shop, to eat and to do the basic essentials of life.

And those have performed very, very well throughout the pandemic and beyond, and investors have realized that. And you have seen good returns experienced in those sectors. But we are also now seeing some of the more discretionary retail perform quite well. And so whether that's Main Street retail on major nodes like in Toronto and Bloor Street, or whether that is in other formats such as enclosed shopping centers-- which really were challenged-- they have begun bouncing back. Not all of them, but a number of them have begun bouncing back.

So retail has performed quite well, and that would be a surprise, I think, to a lot of folks that were looking at the space two to three to four years ago.

Greg Bonnell: Is that success of brick-and-mortar retail coming at the expense of industrials or warehouses?

Colin Lynch: That's a great question. Short answer is, not really. So, how does that work? Well, effectively, we have had a moderately to robustly-- depending on where you're looking in the world-- growing economy. And that manifests itself into more purchases.

And retail versus industrial-- if you look at the industrial, part of industrial that serves those purchases is called logistics. So when you buy, you can buy in person or online. But if the economy is growing, people are spending more, you can actually have both sectors win.

So that has been happening in 2024. Certainly, we saw good strength in both sectors. What's happened in 2025, some questions around the broader macroeconomic, geopolitical, tariff-influenced environment. And that has created a bit of an air pocket for the industrial sector. So not as strong as it was three years ago, four years ago.

Certainly the fundamentals, so i.e., what's the vacancy? Still pretty healthy-- increasing, but pretty healthy. What's the rents? Well, there are three times higher market rents than they were four to five years ago, but they're no longer increasing. And in some cases, they're decreasing.

And so, you step back and you say, is that sector still strong, generally? Does it make sense to have all your chips in that one sector bucket? Probably not. But it didn't make sense three years ago. So we do think diversification really matters. And this is an instance-- whether it's geopolitical uncertainty and the tariff environment-- where we say, yeah, it's interesting, it is a relevant sector, but so are all the other sectors in real estate as well.

Greg Bonnell: We got to get to the residential part of that bucket. Obviously, this has been an interesting space because we're told we have a shortage of homes, but we also know we're cutting back on the welcoming of the number of new Canadians we're welcoming. How does that play out?

Colin Lynch: Yeah, that's right. And I'd say those two pieces are related to each other in terms of the changes to the immigration policies were certainly influenced by the notion of a housing shortage. I would just add, we have to be very clear on where precisely this shortage is. The shortage is in affordable housing.

Where the shortage does not exist, I would argue-- where a surplus of housing exists-- is in luxury accommodations. And so, where do we see luxury accommodations? Certainly, a lot of the condominium units that were sold and are being constructed right now in Toronto, in Vancouver, for instance.

And over the course of this year, in Toronto alone we'll have tens of thousands of new condominium units delivered. And as you look at that market, you're seeing price declines, you're seeing higher vacancy. But on the other hand, if you look at some of the more affordable apartments, you see extremely low vacancy and tremendous demand. And then if you go further and you look at social housing, you see tremendous wait lists and no vacancy.

And so, when we talk about a housing crisis, which is true-- we are in one-- we have to define where that housing crisis exists, which is in affordable housing. And if you have the privilege of serving that sector-- as we do-- by providing, in many instances, affordable housing for Canadians, and we have housing outside of Canada as well, what we have seen is consistently low vacancy.

We've seen actually turnover drop because people want to stay in their units, but we've been also able to accomplish rental growth because we have provided good spaces for people to live in.

Greg Bonnell: Now, real estate, big part of your focus obviously. We've been talking about you're also looking of, obviously, an alternative. There has been a lot talk about that in this kind of environment. A lot of uncertainty, too, going on. I mean, how do you sit back and watch what happened-- I think even the past six weeks-- the roller coaster ride we've been on?

Colin Lynch: That's a good question. So certainly, alternatives, I like to call it the private markets. So these are non-publicly traded investments. And I've been asked how I feel. And I respond by saying I've been very calm and tranquil. And why do I say that? Because at the end of the day, our job is to understand, has something changed?

And if it has changed, our job is to get ahead of the market and implement changes to portfolios so that we can build and grow income long term and we can achieve exceptional performance. So the question is, through all the turbulence and all the volatility and all the announcements, what has fundamentally changed?

So, in the infrastructure space, for instance, a lot of questions about the environmental side of things, and particularly green energy, renewable energy, and the relevancy of that.

I'd step back and say our power needs as a global society will nearly double by 2050. And so how will we be able to produce and supply that amount of energy? Renewable energy will be part of that answer.

As we look at trade-- whether it's consumption or production of goods-- that we have had ports since the beginning of civilization and will continue to have ports over the long term. And they will continue to import a variety of goods-- not just consumer goods but manufacturing goods, goods demanded by governments in a variety of different forms. Think defense. And so they will continue to be relevant long term.

Now, does that volatility produce buying opportunities? Absolutely. And so we're constantly on the lookout for those. Does that volatility create long-term shifts and the potential thereof? Absolutely. So we're always evaluating that. But our job is to not get transfixed by the short term. Our job is really to understand what is the impact long term and position portfolios accordingly.

Original Post

data.title: Apple: The U.S. Darling Bullied By The President
data.description:
Introduction

Oh boy, here we go again. Nothing compares to that feeling when, after a relatively quiet couple of weeks, President Trump apparently thought that he hadn’t made enough news recently and hadn’t rattled the markets for too long. Thus, he decides to reignite the trade war on not just Europe with a potential 50% tariff, but also on one of America’s darlings, Apple Inc. (NASDAQ:AAPL). I will go through what this means for the company, and the consumers, and whether I think this is just another “Art of the Deal” moment from the current administration.

Targeted attack on Apple

So far, during these last few months, Trump has been rather wide-reaching when it comes to the proposed tariffs. No country was spared in his actions. Cue the famous quote from Oprah, “You get tariffs, and you get tariffs, everyone gets tariffs!”

It’s been rather quiet on this front for a couple of weeks now. Market indices more or less recovered from the tariff nonsense of the past, which brought the S&P 500 (SPY) back to almost all-time highs once again. The tariff rhetoric settled down, everyone thought. It’s over. The recession is canceled. Then, just when everyone got comfortable, Trump decided to drop another bombshell right before the weekend. Cruel indeed.

But this time it’s different. Instead of targeting countries, Trump announced that Apple will be facing 25% tariffs on iPhones that are not produced in the U.S., whether they are produced in China or India. That is the first time the President is singling out one company, the darling of the U.S.

This raises a lot of questions in terms of legality and policy. Under the current U.S. trade law, it is not specifically prohibited, especially if it is framed like a tariff on a particular product, and not on the company itself, but when the company, like Apple, makes a big chunk of its revenue from iPhones, if I was Tim “Apple” Cook, I would be feeling somewhat targeted.

Effect on Apple

Since the company heavily relies on a robust global supply chain, in this case, primarily China and India, for the production of the iPhone, many analysts believe that moving the production line to the U.S. is unrealistic, to say the least. It is just too complex to arrange such moves in the short-to-mid-term. It is very costly, time-consuming, and risky because attempting to replicate the supply chain Apple built over the years in cheaper countries is going to be impossible.

Analysts say that if the company is to absorb the 25% tariff while staying with its current supply chain, AAPL’s profits will take a hit. That’s a no-brainer. They estimate that the impact will be around $0.51 per share on its annual earnings. However, all the companies before AAPL in this environment have already announced that they are not willing to absorb any of these costs due to the tariffs and will be passing them on to the consumers. Apple will do just that, too. I don’t think the company is going to bow down and suddenly pick up its production lines and move into the U.S. It’s not feasible, which means Tim Cook will most likely come up with some sort of deal in the future to mitigate this targeted attack on the company or just increase the prices on its iPhones going forward.

Effect on Consumers

Speaking of price increases. Many analysts believe that if the company somehow decides to comply and move its manufacturing to the U.S., which is seen as a “fairy tale” by many, iPhones will cost anywhere from $1,500 to $3,500, from the current $1,190 on the new models.

The 25% tariff is, of course, going to lead to a significant price increase for American consumers. Trump has no power to order/bully Tim Cook around to absorb the impact of tariffs, no matter how many billions in bottom line the company made, which was almost $25B in the latest quarter. CNET did a lot of the heavy work in showing what the effect of the proposed 25% tariff will be on the consumer on many of the models that are currently available, and it is not surprising at all. When it comes to the Chinese-made handsets, it is quite grim, to be honest. Even the older models would be priced at a significant premium. More than the newer current model, even.

We can also see numbers regarding Indian-made handsets, which are not as bad, but still a massive increase, nonetheless.

Recently, even before all this morning’s drama, consumers were panic buying handsets at Apple Stores before the previous tariffs took effect. Now, with this additional tariff, I expect more panic buying to ensue, which in a way is going to help boost the company’s performance in the short term.

So, this brings me to another question and to a swift answer based on consumer behavior: how sensitive are Apple consumers to a change in prices? Panic buying suggests, quite sensitive. There is a limit to what even the hardest of fans of the company and its ecosystem will be willing to cough up for a new phone model.

Closing Comments

The time of waiting on the sidelines is back on. The uncertainty of what is going to happen over the next few weeks is a bit too much to bet on a good outcome. We have to look at the broader implications here. Putting a tariff on iPhones made in China and India could also have much broader implications for the whole trade situation between these countries. Can the president single out just the iPhones manufactured in China and India, or will we see such a blanket tariff on all goods manufactured in these countries? It is too early to tell, and I am sure there will be a lot of volatility going forward.

In the end, I believe the president will flip-flop, as he has in the past. The same goes for his proposed 50% tariff on Europe. He thinks it’s his leverage, the "Art of the Deal." Spook the markets a little, and paused tariffs indefinitely once again, ease the markets, and when he feels he needs more limelight, repeat the process.

So, it is quite a tricky time for investment once again. Be cautious where you put your money right now, especially if you don’t have full conviction, as I do not have in Apple right now. I’m rooting for a drop in prices further so that I can start a position once again in the darling of America.

data.title: Axon: The Cost Of Safety Makes This Innovator Worth The Price
data.description:
I wrote about Axon (NASDAQ:AXON) as early as mid-2024. It was expensive then, and it is expensive now. However, going through the latest information, it's clear that the company continues to earn its premium valuation. Its management has demonstrated the ability to continue and accelerate its growth trajectory through innovative new hardware and software products.

Axon’s Latest Quarter Results Continue to Impress

What I have been impressed with by Axon is how its revenues continue to rise at a strong, healthy clip. The company, despite seeming like being in the hardware defense/industrials industry, is putting out growth numbers similar to a software SaaS company. Last year marked the 3rd consecutive year of more than 30% annual revenue growth, and at least the beginning of this year, Axon seems to be following this growth trend.

The company has showcased strong results in Q1 2025 as revenue rose by 31% year-over-year to $604 million. Management has attributed this growth to increased demand for TASER 10, Axon Body 4, and software and sensor solutions. Digging deeper into the company’s financials, it can be seen that software is an increasingly strong component of the company’s revenues. As I’ve discussed in some of my previous articles, Axon’s business model involves selling the hardware and then supplying value-added software needed on a recurring basis. It's similar to a classic “razor and blade” model but with a SAAS twist.

Therefore, as Axon’s hardware sales go up, via its TASER and body cam products, the more opportunity management has to cross-sell its value-added software solutions. In other words, Axon’s software fees are a form of recurring revenue. So I was pleased that Axon’s Software & Services was up 39% year over year, generating $263 million in revenue.

More impressively, Axon’s Annual Recurring Revenue (ARR) grew by 34% year-over-year to $1.1 billion, with a net revenue retention of 123%. Having a net revenue retention above 100% means that current customers are making additional purchases, either via cross-sales or net additions. Therefore, customers are continuing to find increasing value with the company’s growing suite of product offerings. These results, in my view, put Axon up there with among the best software SaaS companies.

Apart from software, in Q1 2025, Axon has also shown impressive results in hardware sales. All hardware categories showed a healthy growth rate with TASER revenue growing by 19%, Personal Sensors, i.e., Body cameras growing by 30%, and the new Platform Solutions category, which includes VR training and counter drones, growing by 51%. While the TASER category is still the largest in terms of total revenue at $195 million or 57% of total hardware sales, the other two categories are quickly catching up. What is key to remember, though, is that hardware sales don’t only bring in value in themselves, but rather expand the user base for Axon’s total product suite.

New Vehicle Intelligence Platform, Another Leg of Possible Growth

In order to retain these high retention rates and expand its user base, Axon has to continue to develop new products and features that will delight its law enforcement customers. It's with this in mind that the company has recently launched Axon Outpost and Axon Lightpost cameras as part of its vehicle intelligence solutions. Out of the company’s most recent product announcements, in my view, these are the most exciting as it expands the company’s ecosystem to include vehicle surveillance.

Basically, these devices are “intelligent cameras” that can capture things like license plate recognition, and for the more advanced Axon Outpost other vehicle attributes such as color, and visible damage. Placing these in existing city infrastructure, such as street lights and corners, could help police officers by giving them a more complete view of the situation in real-time.

It's actually pretty smart how they designed and deployed this system. The more advanced system is Axon Outpost, which combines livestreaming, license plate recognition, and vehicle recognition all in one device. Basically, everything a cop would need to identify and chase down a suspect. The Axon Lightpost, on the other hand, is a simpler device built for mass deployment. The device can be hoisted on an already existing streetlight without the need for any new infrastructure. However, despite being fast and easy to deploy, it still has powerful features such as license plate recognition and livestreaming.

Axon Fuses, which serves as the next-generation vehicle intelligence platform, ties this all together. It functions as a centralized intelligence hub that aggregates video feeds from not only Axon’s new Outpost and Lightpost products but also Axon’s other devices and even third-party cameras from other manufacturers. This basically transforms individual, siloed cameras into a full-scale surveillance network. I believe that this product is very value-adding to law enforcement customers of Axon and further strengthens the overall ecosystem. Initiatives like these make me confident that Axon can maintain its high retention rate as well as drive new user growth in the quarters ahead.

Tariff Risk is Now a Reality

In my previous article written earlier this year, I highlighted how President Trump’s tariffs could be a potential issue for the company. Now, what was once a distant fear has now become a terrifying reality. As seen recently, the Trump administration has not been shy on imposing hefty tariffs on other countries, despite the impacts on the economy and supply chains. Luckily, Axon’s management has worked hard in recent years to diversify its supplier base and bring some of that manufacturing work back to the United States. As mentioned in the latest earnings call, “…we already manufacture our TASER devices in Arizona, but we do have an impact on the supply chain. The team has done a great job ensuring we can be flexible and nimble, and we think we are well positioned to manage through the shifting environment”.

The other factor that could help mitigate the potential impact of tariffs on Gross margins due to higher cost of goods sold is the software mix of earnings. As mentioned above, software is starting to make up a larger portion of the company’s revenues. In Q1 2025, Axon’s adjusted gross margin increased by 40 basis points to 63.6% year-over-year, attributable to a higher software mix. That being said, I don’t expect there to be a continuous increase in margins every quarter. This is because, like the Axon Outpost and Axon Lightpost described above, the company continues to create and release brand-new hardware products. However, I do expect that increasing software sales will help the company mitigate some tariff impact on earnings.

The final mitigating factor is, as I’ve discussed in my previous article, unlike a lot of companies, Axon’s main customers are primarily governments. Government budgets work differently from what we see in the private sector. Therefore, on the international front, a tariff on Axon’s goods wouldn’t really matter if it’s purchased by a government entity. I’ve detailed how this works in my previous article, basically, “It will be just like putting money from one pocket to another. Let me explain, if a foreign government would purchase Axon’s body cameras and levy an additional 10% tariff on those cameras, it would be paying the additional 10% tariff to itself as tax”. I am optimistic that this means that the tariffs would not end up hurting Axon so much.

Valuation and Conclusion

All this being said, I really like Axon as a company. I feel their management is on point, and they continue to surprise me with their new and innovative products. Unsurprisingly, the company has scored an “F” in its valuation metrics, with its Non-GAAP forward P/E of 121.54 and forward EV/Sales of 25.83.

That being said, Axon can remain highly valued for extended periods of time. If you look at Palantir Technologies (PLTR), its valuations are even higher than Axon, with a forward Non-GAAP P/E of 216x. In some ways, Palantir is pretty similar to Axon as it is a “safety” oriented firm, but in this case, helping the military against external threats. This all circles back to the cost to governments of keeping their population safe. Something that it seems these days has a budget that is ever-increasing. Axon is well-positioned to capture a large portion of those government contracts.

It’s easy and tempting to write off and downgrade Axon because of its “expensive price.” However, it is a company that continues to innovate and grow its Total Addressable Market. This is evidenced by its Vehicle monitoring solution, which can grow into an entirely new business segment and has massive potential. Furthermore, Axon’s main customers (i.e., governments) have a nearly unlimited pool of money, which it is not shy about spending in the name of defense and safety. I have decided to keep my “Buy” rating for this stock with the intention of accumulating slowly or buying the dips.

data.title: Southwest Airlines: No Inflection Point
data.description:
Airline stocks have not been performing well year-to-date, and Southwest Airlines Co. (NYSE:LUV) is no exception, having declined 6.5%. Interestingly, year-to-date Southwest Airlines, while down 6.5%, is still performing significantly better than its peers. I believe that this is primarily driven by its focus on the domestic market shielding it to some extent from hesitance on international travel.

Since my last report in December, the stock has lost 9.3% compared to a 4% loss for the S&P 500 (SP500). The stock lost altitude following comments from CFO Tom Doxey during the Wolfe Global Transportation and Industrials Conference. In this report, I briefly look at the key observations from the conference and update my price target and rating.

No Inflection Point Going Into The Summer

The reason why Southwest Airlines stock is down can probably best be captured in this comment from Tom Doxey:

...to give maybe just a little bit of context. Yes, So 1Q unit revenue was about three points worse than we were expecting at the beginning of the year. 2Q is probably about six points worse than what we were expecting at the beginning of the year, right? And we've of course guided for that. And we have a lot of data. We have a lot of industry data and we have not seen in the industry an inflection back, right?

That Q1 and Q2 were worse than initially expected was already clear. I do not consider that part of the comment to be a major surprise. However, the more downbeat part of the comment is that Southwest Airlines has not seen an inflection point. That simply seems to indicate that things have not gotten better going into the summer season which is when airlines harvest earnings.

On capacity, the company expects to increase by 1 to 2 percent this year with 1.5% capacity taken out of the system in H2 2025. The company has 50 Boeing (BA) planes set for retirement this year while introducing 38 new airplane deliveries from Boeing. The company sees some improved visibility in airplane deliveries which could result in taking more deliveries this year. When that happens, Southwest Airlines will retire more airplanes. So, we are definitely at a spot right now where some airlines are looking to keep the fleet stable rather than growing it out. That makes a lot of sense when considering the fragile market conditions.

Focus On EBIT Improvement Continues

In September 2024, Southwest Airlines stock jumped on the company’s aim to increase EBIT to $4 billion. I wasn’t quite impressed with that as the company seemed to be picking the low-hanging fruit through better turn times, the introduction of redeye flights, and a new fare system and better cabin segmentation. You could really wonder why the company had not done so earlier.

When I discussed the initiatives the company is working on in September, charging for bags seemed to be out of question. The company has walked away from the bags fly-free policy, and that should drive revenues. On the cost side, the company is expecting run rate savings of $1 billion by 2027 compared to $500 million initially expected.

So, the company is bolstering its cost reduction effort and has an additional lever to drive revenues as it now seems to have adapted to industry realities whereas it first believed charging for backs would provide a net negative to the business.

The company will be launching its new fare product and bag charges next week, followed by seat assignment and extra legroom being marketed after that. These changes should be positives but come during market conditions that are not quite favorable for airlines. Adding that there is a ramp involved in seeing the impact of the initiatives and a clear signal of the impact on overall financials may be weak this year but potentially be stronger into the next.

No Strong Buy Signal For Southwest Airlines

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener that uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows, and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions, and the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

For Southwest Airlines, I do not see a strong buy signal. EBITDA was higher in 2024 than expected, but estimates for 2025 have been lowered by around 5% for a total reduction in EBITDA between 2024 and 2027 of 3%. For 2026, earnings are expected to snap back, but we do note that there is uncertainty in the market and that makes it difficult to pull 2026 into the valuation. Therefore, I am maintaining my hold rating for the stock. My assumption on share repurchases is that rather than utilizing the full $2.25 billion in share repurchases this year, it will be spread over two years while debt will be balanced to stick to liquidity levels of $4 billion.

I will be looking forward to the company’s comment during the Q2 earnings call on its share repurchase plans for this year under the current market conditions. I believe that under the current market conditions, it is prudent to delay the share repurchases.

Conclusion: Southwest Airlines Barely Impresses In Downbeat Market

I was not quite impressed with the cost and revenue initiatives that Southwest Airlines presented last year. It should help the company realize better results, but I believe that in large these changes were long overdue and some of these changes are now only providing an offset to the weaker market conditions. The company has taken capacity out of the system in the back half of the year. However, due to the weak market conditions we are seeing, the improvements in business optimization are being masked by an uncertain market environment. This leaves a lot of the results to become visible in 2026 when more optimizations are rendered and the market hopefully is in a healthier spot.

data.title: Autodesk Q1: Focusing On AI And Cloud
data.description:
I assigned a Buy rating to Autodesk, Inc. (NASDAQ:ADSK) in December 2024, highlighting its strong billing growth. I favor the company’s focus on cloud, platform, and AI technologies. I reiterate a Buy rating with a fair value of $336 per share.

Autodesk released its Q1 FY 2026 result on May 22nd, delivering 16% constant revenue growth and 24.1% adjusted operating profit growth, as depicted in the table below. Notably, the company delivered strong growth across different regions with robust margin expansion.

My key takeaway from the quarter was the management’s focus on cloud, platform and AI technologies. During the earnings call, the management highlighted the standardization of Autodesk Construction Cloud, which connects workflows and teams and provides better collaboration across designers and construction management. I think these investments in cloud and AI could help Autodesk sustain competitive advantages in the Architecture, Engineering, and Construction (AEC) market.

In addition, Autodesk launched the AI AutoConstrain for Fusion in February 2025, simplifying the design process with AI features. During the call, the management noted that this feature has created over 580,000 constraints for their Fusion users since it launched. I am encouraged by these AI functionalities, which could power Autodesk’s traditional software solutions. In other words, AI provides an excellent add-on to their existing platforms and solutions.

In addition, the company repurchased $354 million of own shares in Q1 FY 2026, compared to only $9 million in Q1 FY 2025. The accelerating shares repurchase demonstrates the company’s improved financial situation and better capital allocation policies.

For FY 2026, Autodesk is guiding for 14%-15% constant revenue growth and 20%-22% billing growth, as detailed in the table below. Considering their strong Q1 result, I think the guidance is quite reasonable.

Benchmark forecasts that the global AEC market will grow at a CAGR of 10.3% from 2023 to 2032, driven by digital manufacturing and designing activities. I think Autodesk’s business growth will continue to benefit from digitalization, cloud and AI applications. The combination of AI and traditional AEC platform could potentially simply designers’ work and provide better collaborations between designers and constructions. In the near-term, I anticipate Autodesk will deliver 11% organic revenue growth, slightly higher than the overall market growth. In addition, I anticipate the company will allocate 8% of revenue towards M&A, contributing an additional 3% growth to the topline. As such, I forecast the company will deliver 14% year-over-year growth in total revenue.

I model 40bps annual margin expansion, driven by 20bps from reductions of stock options and 20bps from SG&A operating leverage. During the call, the management emphasized their target of lowering stock option compensations (SBC) to below 10% as a percentage of total revenue. I calculate the total operating expenses will grow by 12% annually, leading to 40bps annual margin expansion.

DCF Summary

FCFF Calculation

The WACC is calculated to be 10% assuming: Risk-free 4.2%; beta 1; equity risk premium 6%; cost of debt 5%; equity $63 billion; debt $2.2 billion; tax rate 18%. I set the terminal growth rate to be 5.5%, aligned with the overall software growth rate. Discounting all the future FCFF and adjusting the net cash, the fair value is calculated to be $336 per share, as per my estimates.

Key Risk

During the call, the management expressed that the tariff uncertainty has caused some negative impact on their customers. It’s worth noting that Autodesk’s end-customers are across a broad range of markets including manufacturing, construction and industrials. The tariff uncertainty might cause their end-customers to cut costs for IT spendings. Having said that, Autodesk’s full-year guidance has already incorporated the macroeconomic uncertainties.

Conclusion

I believe the company’s strategic focus on cloud and AI could maintain their competitive advantages among the AEC market. I reiterate a Buy rating with a fair value of $336 per share.

data.title: Primoris Services: A Rising Star In The Infrastructure Arena
data.description:
Company Description

Primoris Services Corporation (NYSE:PRIM) provides important infrastructure services including construction, maintenance, and engineering for companies in the utility and energy sectors across the United States and Canada. The company sports a current market cap of about $4B and employs slightly more than 15,000 people. The stock has done very well over the past decade, achieving a total return of nearly 300%, resulting in a CAGR of 14.6%.

Financial Metrics

I believe there are three important quantitative characteristics all high-quality businesses should possess; a growing revenue stream, a reliable gross profit margin, and a robust return on invested capital.

Over the past decade, PRIM's revenue per share has grown at a robust pace. From 2015 through 2020, the RPS rose by a little more than 14% per year. In 2021, the company issued 4.5M shares to finance the acquisition of Future Infrastructure Holdings, contributing to a decline in the RPS metric of 8.1%. Since then, however, Primoris has achieved an impressive RPS growth rate of more than 20% annually.

The gross profit margin has remained relatively stable over the past 10 years, typically ranging between 10% and 12%. The company had a chart low of 10.1% in FY16 and a chart high of 11.9% in FY21.

The return on invested capital for PRIM was low in FY15 and FY16, hovering around 5%. In the subsequent years, the ROIC climbed closer to 10%, before slipping a few percentage points. In FY22, the ROIC decreased further but has since risen in the last two fiscal years.

Overall, these metrics reflect strong performance, particularly the revenue per share metric, which has grown at a solid pace. Additionally, while the GPM has been stable, the ROIC has been more volatile but has shown signs of trending upward.

Dividend Data

Currently, Primoris pays a quarterly dividend of $0.08 per share, resulting in an annual dividend of $0.32 and a corresponding dividend yield of just 0.43%. As indicated by the blue line on the chart, the company raised its dividend for the first time last year in more than half a decade. Although there's been no announcement from management of future consistent dividend increases; hopefully that's something they intend on implementing, especially given the low/decreasing payout ratio as represented by the orange line below.

Q1 2025 Earnings

Earlier this month, Primoris announced Q1 2025 earnings with GAAP EPS of $0.81 per share, topping expectations by $0.35, on revenue of $1.65B which also exceeded the consensus by nearly $160M. Normalized EPS also surpassed analyst expectations by $0.32, coming in at $0.98 per share.

Looking at the Utilities segment first, revenue rose by $75.5M YoY, or about 15%. This dramatic increase occurred as a result of increased activity in power delivery, communications, and gas markets. Furthermore, operating income grew by $18M due to sales growth, coupled with expanding gross margins.

The Energy segment saw sales increase by $160M, or 17%, compared to the same period a year ago thanks to increased renewable energy adoption. Additionally, operating income also grew by $12.3M, more than 18% due to strong revenue growth.

Lastly, management reaffirmed its FY25 estimates, with net income expected to be in the range of $203.3 to $214.3 million. While EPS is projected to be between $3.70 and $3.90, and adjusted EPS between $4.20 and $4.40.

In the approximate two weeks since their earnings report, the stock has climbed nearly 15%. The full earnings call can be read/listened to here.

Risks to Consider

Generally, I believe PRIM is a solid company headed in the right direction; however, I think it's important to consider some potential headwinds the company is or could face.

The current macroeconomic environment consisting of a variety of issues, most notably uncertainty surrounding tariffs (although that has somewhat subsided recently), could lead to potential challenges for PRIM. In fact, in the company's Q4 2024 earnings release management acknowledged tariffs as a headwind, stating they could increase costs and/or delay projects, particularly in the Energy segment. If this issue persists, it could hinder profitability, even with impressive increases in revenue and a record backlog of nearly $12B.

Also discussed in their Q4 2024 earnings release was their skilled labor shortage. In the report, the company mentioned rising labor costs as a challenge they continually face, particularly in the Utilities segment. And although revenue in this segment climbed by more than 10%, its margins shrank, with increased labor costs stated as one of the causes. With ongoing competition for skilled labor, not just for PRIM but all companies in the construction industry, we're likely to see rising wages which could ultimately affect profitability as well as delay projects.

Valuation

Let's review Primoris from a free cash flow perspective to decide if the company is trading for an attractive valuation. Before we dive into that, let me add a little more context on how to view the chart below. The estimated fair value is calculated by dividing the FCF/share for that year by the trailing 5-year average of the FCF yield. The discount line is 10% below the estimated FV, while the premium is 10% above, and lastly, the Trim line is 40% above the approximate FV. Finally, you'll see the chart goes slightly into 2025, and because no complete 2025 FCF/share data is available the TTM FCF per share is used.

The valuation model suggests PRIM is trading for a significant discount to its fair value, more than 30%, giving the stock a potential fair value of $110. Aside from 2020, and the last fiscal year, the FCF for Primoris hasn't fluctuated a ton. In 2020, the FCF/share surged to $5.13 per share, compared to just $0.46 the prior year. And in a couple of years post-pandemic, the company incurred a few negative years of FCF, before turning positive once again in 2023. The FCF grew significantly in 2024 due to a multitude of factors including; robust revenue growth, record operating cash flow, and expanding margins.

The long-term expected rate of return for Primoris sits at an impressive 19.21%; with the components of that estimate being a 0.43% dividend yield, a 5.71% return to fair value factor, as well as an expected earnings growth rate of 13.07%.

In summary, the stock has performed well over the past decade, slightly outpacing the broad market. The company's financials are pretty decent too, including dramatic revenue growth of 26.4%, 29.3%, and 11.4% from 2022 through 2024. As a dividend investor, it's a little frustrating there hasn't been more dividend growth, but I'm hopeful we'll see some more consistent increases in the coming years. The recent earnings report was encouraging, showing a significant amount of growth in both segments of the company, as well as a growing backlog. Based on the model, the stock is undervalued by quite a bit, and thus I rate Primoris a buy.

data.title: Hysan Development: Eyes On Debt Refinancing And Portfolio Optimization
data.description:
My mixed view of Hysan Development Company Limited (OTCPK:HYSNY) (OTCPK:HYSNF) (0014.HK) or "HDCL" is unchanged.

The previous October 23, 2024 article evaluated HDCL's balance sheet strength and cash distributions.

I'm worried about the unfavorable impact of the company's refinancing move in the near future. However, its prospects could improve in the intermediate term with asset divestiture and property expansion. A Hold rating for HYSNY is warranted.

HYSNF's New Debt Is More Expensive

HDCL previously had $0.75B of perpetual securities (perp) with a call option exercisable in June this year.

The group subsequently issued a fresh perp in Q1'25 to refinance the old one in full. But this new debt carries a coupon rate that is 310 bps (basis points) higher at 7.2%.

I estimate that HYSNF could incur additional finance expenses of over HKD 150M on annualized terms.

In my opinion, HYSNY's future results and capital returns will be under pressure.

At its earlier February 18 analyst briefing (S&P Capital IQ transcript), HDCL disclosed a "positive mid-single-digit" percentage "improvement" for both its shopping malls' 2M25 "footfall" and "tenant revenues". The HK (Hong Kong) city as a whole also witnessed a 3.2% sequential growth in March '25 retail sales.

HYSNF's business prospects are getting better, but this might be negated by the heavier interest burden.

My forecasts suggest that HYSNY's normalized EPS will decline by 10% to HK$1.71 in the full year 2025. This represents a reversal from the company's 7% bottom-line expansion last year.

Also, I'm pessimistic about its dividend outlook.

DPS (Dividend Per Share) for HDCL fell by 25% to HKD 1.08 in FY23.

HYSNF was able to maintain an unchanged distribution for FY2024. But its cash payout in the prior year amounted to HK$1,100M. This was substantially above the group's FCF (Free Cash Flow) of HKD 0.7B during the same period.

I have concerns about the sustainability of HYSNY's dividends, especially since it is incurring higher interest costs in the current year. My prediction is that HDCL's FY25 DPS could be reduced by 8% to HK$1.00.

HYSNF's pricier debt is a major negative.

HDCL Has Plans To Reshape Its Portfolio

It's hard to imagine HYSNY's shares rising in the short-term when dividend cuts and earnings contraction are possibly on the cards.

But if you had a longer time horizon, the company is taking specific actions to boost its medium-term performance.

One thing to watch is that HYSNF has the intent to monetize certain assets.

The group has a Hong Kong residential property project called "Bamboo Grove" (BG). A check on a domestic real estate agent's portal indicated that HYSNY had listed two of BG's buildings for sale in March.

My calculations suggest that the successful disposal of the above-mentioned would generate around HKD 4B of proceeds. This figure is determined based on the implied psf (per square foot) valuation of around HK$20,000 as per the list price.

HDCL's adjusted leverage (net debt-to-equity with perps taken as borrowings) is 0.50x now. If the company does divest these assets, its gearing could be lowered to a more comfortable mid-40s percentage. This in turn translates into a decrease in finance costs and higher profit.

Separately, HDCL is in the process of implementing its multi-year plan to restructure the "Lee Gardens" or "LG" property in HK's Causeway Bay as outlined in the chart below.

LG Asset Enhancement Timeline

The initial results of this transformation have been encouraging. HYSNF's retail revenues grew by 10% in FY2024. This came about as over ten luxury brands opened new shops in the revamped LG.

New "LG8" (Lee Gardens Eight) Development

As per the diagram (bottom-right-hand corner) presented above, HYSNY is constructing a new mixed-use asset LG8 which is scheduled for completion in Q2'26. By then, LG's total square footage would have increased by close to a third, implying a meaningful jump in future rental income.

A Description Of LG8's Appeal To Potential Tenants

It's easy to deliver superior returns when you take a hard look at your business portfolio. Adding value to the crown jewel such as LG and parting ways with non-core investments like BG are the wise actions that HYSNF has taken.

My Closing Thoughts

I still assign a Hold to Hysan now.

HYSNY isn't deserving of a Buy. There are downside risks associated with the recent refinancing.

But I wouldn't be bearish on HYSNF either. There are opportunities for the group to make favorable changes to its collection of assets.

The GGM (Gordon Growth Model) Formula

HDCL's valuations are aligned with the current returns it has achieved. I'm assuming a 2.6% ROE (actual FY24 results), a 1% perpetuity growth rate (g pegged to the mature economy's modest expansion), and a 9% cost of equity (market average k). Using these inputs and the equation above, I arrive at a 0.2x target P/B. This is the same as what the stock is trading at.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
